title: TankerTrackers: Sanctioned tanker breaches US blockade with AIS broadcasting along Oman coast
summary: TankerTrackers reports a US-sanctioned supertanker breached the US naval blockade while keeping its AIS transponder operational along the Omani coast. The platform also tracks up to 20 empty supertankers available for loading or floating storage, four of which remain outside US sanctions, plus a VLCC carrying 2 million barrels still anchored inside Iranian waters.
contents:
# Sanctioned tanker breaches US blockade with AIS on along Oman coast — TankerTrackers

## Overview
TankerTrackers reports that a US-sanctioned supertanker breached the maritime blockade declared by Washington while keeping its Automatic Identification System (AIS) operational along the coast of Oman, according to a Voice of Emirates summary published April 28, 2026. Analysts interpret the move as a deliberate public test of US maritime deterrence and Washington's capacity to enforce the blockade.

## Key data points
- 1 supertanker: completed a blockade-line crossing with AIS broadcasting openly along the Oman coast
- Up to 20 empty supertankers: currently available, possibly for loading Iranian crude or for floating storage
- 4 of those 20: outside US sanctions, giving Tehran a narrow legal margin for ownership transfers at sea
- 1 VLCC carrying ~2 million barrels: still inside Iranian waters, anchored further west than expected

## AIS-on transit interpreted as deterrence test
The deliberate decision to keep AIS active during a sanctioned voyage along the Omani coast contrasts sharply with the ghost-fleet pattern of dark transits and transponder spoofing. Analysts view the open broadcast as a demonstration that Iranian operators no longer fear meaningful US enforcement cost. Reuters previously documented several sanctioned supertankers entering the Arabian Gulf despite Washington's warnings, and TankerTrackers data corroborates that pattern.

## Floating storage build-up
Up to 20 empty supertankers position Tehran to absorb surplus crude resulting from supply chain disruptions or to stage future loadings once windows of opportunity open. Four unsanctioned hulls within that pool give Iran flexibility for ownership shuffles, bareboat charters, or ship-to-ship transfers without immediate OFAC exposure.

## Tactical positioning
TankerTrackers also notes that a supertanker holding roughly two million barrels of crude has not yet exited Iranian waters and now sits at a more western anchorage. The shift suggests an evolving Iranian fleet posture under mounting international pressure, with operators apparently waiting for a political breakthrough or a breach in the naval blockade.

## Significance
The combined behavior — AIS-on blockade run, empty supertanker build-up, unsanctioned tonnage, and tactical anchoring — points to a coherent Iranian campaign of resistance tactics that probes US enforcement boundaries while preserving optionality. Each successful crossing erodes deterrence credibility and emboldens shadow-fleet operators across the region.

## Tags
tanker-tracking, iran, oman, blockade, sanctions, ais, dark-fleet, shadow-fleet, vlcc, deterrence
